questions.main_text: _Write a composition of between 150 and 250 words on one of the following topics. Follow the guidance._

content.text: _Write a composition of between 150 and 250 words on one of the following topics. Follow the guidance._

questions.main_text:
**1.1g The Counterfeit**

​

The visitors came in their thousands, staring intently at the Rembrandts, Rubenses and Murillos in the hope that they would be able to spot the imposter hidden among the old masters. Now, three months since Dulwich Picture Gallery challenged the public by replacing one of their collection masterpieces with a Chinese replica, the counterfeit has finally been revealed. Since 10 February, Jean-Honoré Fragonard’s 18th-century work “Young Woman” has been replaced by a £70 handpainted replica, produced in China and ordered over the internet, which has hung amid 270 old masters. 

​

The exhibition, titled Made in China, was conceived by the American artist Doug Fishbone to encourage people to think about the way they look, appreciate and value such artistic masterpieces. The gallery’s chief curator, Xavier Bray, said: “It was certainly quite stimulating because it turns everything you assume you know upside down. A museum is a temple of art and as soon as you cross the threshold you expect everything you are told on a label to be correct. So suddenly having this intervention from a contemporary artist that makes you question every piece can be quite unnerving, but in a positive way.” 

​

As it turned out, of the thousands of people who visited the gallery during the experiment, only 10% guessed correctly. Bray added: “Some initially accused us of dumbing everything down but I’m pleased to say it proved the opposite, it actually led people to look afresh.” The key giveaways of the replica, he said, were obvious to those who pay attention to the details. These giveaways ranged from the lack of warmth in the background canvas and the modern pigment of acrylic paints, to the expression on the face of the woman in the replica, which Bray described as “lacking psychology, just empty and flat”. 

​

The original has been put back in its frame and hung beside the replica for the sake of comparison. But Bray said that he would miss the element of fun that the presence of the fake had injected into the gallery. “Weirdly, when it was hanging there, I almost got used to it as a permanent part of this collection,” he said. [CITE source=The Guardian, 29 April 2015]

content.text:
**1.1g The Counterfeit**

​

The visitors came in their thousands, staring intently at the Rembrandts, Rubenses and Murillos in the hope that they would be able to spot the imposter hidden among the old masters. Now, three months since Dulwich Picture Gallery challenged the public by replacing one of their collection masterpieces with a Chinese replica, the counterfeit has finally been revealed. Since 10 February, Jean-Honoré Fragonard’s 18th-century work “Young Woman” has been replaced by a £70 handpainted replica, produced in China and ordered over the internet, which has hung amid 270 old masters. 

​

The exhibition, titled Made in China, was conceived by the American artist Doug Fishbone to encourage people to think about the way they look, appreciate and value such artistic masterpieces. The gallery’s chief curator, Xavier Bray, said: “It was certainly quite stimulating because it turns everything you assume you know upside down. A museum is a temple of art and as soon as you cross the threshold you expect everything you are told on a label to be correct. So suddenly having this intervention from a contemporary artist that makes you question every piece can be quite unnerving, but in a positive way.” 

​

As it turned out, of the thousands of people who visited the gallery during the experiment, only 10% guessed correctly. Bray added: “Some initially accused us of dumbing everything down but I’m pleased to say it proved the opposite, it actually led people to look afresh.” The key giveaways of the replica, he said, were obvious to those who pay attention to the details. These giveaways ranged from the lack of warmth in the background canvas and the modern pigment of acrylic paints, to the expression on the face of the woman in the replica, which Bray described as “lacking psychology, just empty and flat”. 

​

The original has been put back in its frame and hung beside the replica for the sake of comparison. But Bray said that he would miss the element of fun that the presence of the fake had injected into the gallery. “Weirdly, when it was hanging there, I almost got used to it as a permanent part of this collection,” he said. [CITE source=The Guardian, 29 April 2015]

questions.main_text:
**1.1e Earlier Use of **"**African-American**"

​

The term African-American may seem to be a product of recent decades, exploding into common usage in the 1990s after a push from advocates like civil rights activist Jesse Jackson, and enshrined in the Oxford English Dictionary in 2001. The O.E.D.’s entry, revised in 2012, traces the first known occurrence to 1835, in an anti-slavery newspaper. But now, a researcher has discovered a printed reference in an anti-British sermon from 1782 credited to an anonymous “African American,” pushing the origins of the term back to the earliest days of independence.

​

“We think of it as a neutral alternative to older terms, one that resembles Italian-American or Irish-American,” said Fred Shapiro, an associate director at the Yale Law School Library, who found the reference. “It’s a very striking usage to see back in 1782.” Mr. Shapiro, a longtime contributor to the O.E.D. and the editor of The Yale Book of Quotations, found the reference last month in one of his regular sweeps of various online databases that have transformed lexicographic research by gathering vast swaths of historical texts – once scattered across the collections of far-flung libraries and historical societies – in one easily searchable place.

​

One day, Mr. Shapiro typed “African American” into a database of historical newspapers. Up popped an advertisement that appeared in The Pennsylvania Journal on May 15, 1782, announcing: “Two sermons, written by the African American; one on the Capture of Lord Cornwallis, to be SOLD.” With the indispensable help of George Thompson, a retired librarian from New York University, who demonstrated exceptional expertise concerning this matter, Mr. Shapiro found one of the titles – “A Sermon on the Capture of Lord Cornwallis” – and located a copy of it, a 16-page pamphlet, at Houghton Library at Harvard University. The sermon, which crows about the surrender of the British Army at Yorktown the previous year, was acquired by Harvard in 1845. Its author – listed on the title page as “an African American” – is anonymous, identified only as “not having the benefit of a liberal education.” “Was the author a freeman?” Mr. Shapiro said. “A slave? We don’t know.” Katherine C. Martin, the editor of United States dictionaries at Oxford University Press, said the O.E.D.’s researchers were in the process of confirming Mr. Shapiro’s discovery. “It’s very exciting,” she said. “Once we have it nailed down, I would expect we’ll immediately edit our entry.” [CITE source=International New York Times, 23 April 2015]

​

content.text:

questions.main_text:
**1.1f What Do We Mean by Legacy?**

​

Is legacy the same thing as history, or is it something different? We have arrived, both as the physical and emotional beings we are today, as well as in the built environment that we inhabit and the society we occupy, through the legacy of our ancestors. While the daily details of their lives may not constitute history at large, it is worth remembering that human experience essentially remains constant over time: we all experience childhood – both the good and the bad – as well as love, friendship and of course death. Computers and technology might enhance our lives now and offer useful aids in everyday encounters, but, at the end of the day, does a mother losing her son in battle today feel any different to a woman losing her son in the Great War? Unlikely. These human experiences connect us through time and teach us the nature and importance of a good legacy. Few of us will get to play a part in history, but it’s up to all of us to decide what our own legacy will be. Georgian London: Into The Streets by Lucy Inglis is now out and her blog can be found at lucyinglis.com/georgian-london. [CITE source=Psychologies Magazine, March 2015]

content.text:
**1.1f What Do We Mean by Legacy?**

​

Is legacy the same thing as history, or is it something different? We have arrived, both as the physical and emotional beings we are today, as well as in the built environment that we inhabit and the society we occupy, through the legacy of our ancestors. While the daily details of their lives may not constitute history at large, it is worth remembering that human experience essentially remains constant over time: we all experience childhood – both the good and the bad – as well as love, friendship and of course death. Computers and technology might enhance our lives now and offer useful aids in everyday encounters, but, at the end of the day, does a mother losing her son in battle today feel any different to a woman losing her son in the Great War? Unlikely. These human experiences connect us through time and teach us the nature and importance of a good legacy. Few of us will get to play a part in history, but it’s up to all of us to decide what our own legacy will be. Georgian London: Into The Streets by Lucy Inglis is now out and her blog can be found at lucyinglis.com/georgian-london. [CITE source=Psychologies Magazine, March 2015]

questions.main_text:
**Voting age**

​

Write your response to this post on the discussion forum Debatewise:

​

_16-year-olds are not mature enough to vote in elections. The large majority still live at home and go to school. They may have adult bodies, but their minds are still those of children who have to be protected. By 18 they have become much more independent and are able to make their own way in the world. Their political views are likely to be more thoughtful compared to 16-year-olds, who may just copy their parents’ opinions or adopt silly ideas for the sake of rebellion. _– ofage- [CITE source=debatewise.org (7.6.2015)]

questions.main_text:
**Cloud Power: Streaming Video on the Rise**

​

From the now omnipresent fields of streaming music and video, to the nascent “Internet of Things”, expected to grow exponentially with an estimated 20.8 billion devices by 2020, our online world continues to transform our offline lives with ever increasing speed. Internet data is now growing at 20% per year. Big data’s massive growth is expected to continue with the emergence of cheap smartphones: nearly 80% of the planet’s adult population will be connected to the internet by 2020. Internet traffic from mobile devices increased 69% in 2014 alone with the rapid increase of video streaming to mobile devices, and mobile traffic will exceed what is delivered over wired connections by 2018. The primary engine behind all of this growth is consumer traffic, which represents more than 80% of internet traffic currently, and is expected to maintain this high share through 2018. By far and away, the biggest driver of that consumer internet data is online video. Consumers bought nearly 100 million internet-connected TVs in 2014, and the steady increase of video-enabled mobile devices has dramatically changed how and where we watch TV and movies. YouTube, Netflix, Hulu and other video streaming services that have suddenly become a regular staple in our daily lives already make up more than 60% of consumer internet traffic, and that number is expected to grow to 76% by 2018. [CITE source=www.greenpeace.org (15.5.2015, adapted)]

content.text:
**Cloud Power: Streaming Video on the Rise**

​

From the now omnipresent fields of streaming music and video, to the nascent “Internet of Things”, expected to grow exponentially with an estimated 20.8 billion devices by 2020, our online world continues to transform our offline lives with ever increasing speed. Internet data is now growing at 20% per year. Big data’s massive growth is expected to continue with the emergence of cheap smartphones: nearly 80% of the planet’s adult population will be connected to the internet by 2020. Internet traffic from mobile devices increased 69% in 2014 alone with the rapid increase of video streaming to mobile devices, and mobile traffic will exceed what is delivered over wired connections by 2018. The primary engine behind all of this growth is consumer traffic, which represents more than 80% of internet traffic currently, and is expected to maintain this high share through 2018. By far and away, the biggest driver of that consumer internet data is online video. Consumers bought nearly 100 million internet-connected TVs in 2014, and the steady increase of video-enabled mobile devices has dramatically changed how and where we watch TV and movies. YouTube, Netflix, Hulu and other video streaming services that have suddenly become a regular staple in our daily lives already make up more than 60% of consumer internet traffic, and that number is expected to grow to 76% by 2018. [CITE source=www.greenpeace.org (15.5.2015, adapted)]

questions.main_text:
**1.1b Reading between the Lines**

​

In 2007, graphic designer Erik Schmitt, then on the team behind the original Kindle, was given a portion of his late grandfather’s vast and varied library, including poetry, philosophical treatises, and classics. While flipping through the books, Schmitt noticed that many of the fragile pages were covered in his grandfather’s notes: Frenchto-English translations, thoughtful musings, underlined passages. “I was working in technology,” says Schmitt, “but I realized that in our excitement about the future, we were leaving these fascinating artifacts behind.” 

​

Inspired to unearth more marginalia, Schmitt who lives in Berkeley, began spending his Saturdays browsing a local book exchange and a junk store, collecting unique finds. “Within a single book, I’d see some comments written in blue ink and others written in red by someone else,” says Schmitt. “People were having discussions. But once the books are thrown away, those dialogues are lost.” 

​

After two years of hunting through hundreds of books, Schmitt is presenting his most compelling finds on The Pages Project, a new website that he created during his spare time and where visitors can view passages from works like The Essential Tales of Chekhov (above the line “thoughts, desires, and dreams are no longer my own, but belong to some demon who has taken possession of me…” one reader wrote simply, “Oh my!”), and Selected Writings of Gertrude Stein (a favorite of Schmitt’s because someone underlined how many times Stein referred to herself on one page – eight). 

​

The collection currently totals 50 selections, and the site now accepts submissions from fellow bibliophiles. “I’d like people to reflect on what may be lost as we move further into the digital age,” says Schmitt. “This period in our history might never be repeated.” To browse, visit thepagesproject.com. [CITE source=The Oprah Magazine, April 2015]

​

[GAP id=103] as a royal palace and as a fortress against invasion and rebellion, it has also served other [GAP id=104]. It maintains its position as one of Her Majesty’s palaces. Some families live within its walls, a veritable village community, and [GAP id=105] it is no longer a military stronghold or a prison, the ceremonial of the Tower is scrupulously maintained. Every night at 10 o’clock the gates [GAP id=106] in the Ceremony of the Keys, [GAP id=107] its traditional challenges and responses, and after midnight [GAP id=108] is still nowadays possible to get into the Tower only [GAP id=109] the password.

​

[GAP id=110] is the Tower the best known building in Britain, it is also the most [GAP id=111] about. Since the days of Queen Elizabeth I historians, poets, novelists, playwrights and journalists have produced a seemingly endless stream of words about the place and the personalities [GAP id=112]. The learned antiquary John Stow provided the first historical description of the Tower in his Survey of London. [GAP id=113] the Tower was firmly established [GAP id=114] the main attraction of London [GAP id=115] visitors from home and abroad to see the Crown Jewels. [CITE source=Alan Borg, Strange Stories from the Tower of London, London 1976 (adapted)]

questions.main_text:
**Cancer**

​

We think we know what causes cancer: smoking, the sun’s UV rays, tumorcausing genes we inherit from Mom and Dad. But these factors alone can’t explain why cancer in its many forms is poised to [SELECT id=84] heart disease as America’s No. 1 killer within the next few years. That rise has sparked a [SELECT id=85] of research into how much of cancer is [SELECT id=86] our control and how much of it is simply a roll of the genetic dice.

​

Now, in an eye-opening study published in Science, researchers report that the majority of cancer types are the result of pure chance, the product of random genetic mutations that occur when stem cells make mistakes [SELECT id=87] the cell’s DNA.

​

Christian Tomasetti and Dr. Bert Vogelstein found that [SELECT id=88] stem cells there are in certain kind of tissues and [SELECT id=118] frequently they divide, [SELECT id=119] likely that that tissue is to develop cancer over a person’s lifetime. About 65% of cancers are the result of these DNA mistakes made by stem cells. Only a small proportion of a tissue’s cells are stem cells, which are essentially templates for making more tissue. The catch is that this kind of DNA copying is also the process behind cancer, which is triggered by cells that pick [SELECT id=89] mutations in the genes when they divide. The threat of cancer does not however mean you should stop [SELECT id=90] sunscreen or take up smoking. “The biggest fear is that people think they need do nothing. [SELECT id=91] is true,” says Tomasetti, who stresses that while we may not be able to prevent all tumors, we can focus on early detection and [SELECT id=92] lifesaving treatments. [CITE source=Time, 19 January 2015 (adapted)]

content.text:
**Cancer**

​

We think we know what causes cancer: smoking, the sun’s UV rays, tumorcausing genes we inherit from Mom and Dad. But these factors alone can’t explain why cancer in its many forms is poised to [SELECT id=84] heart disease as America’s No. 1 killer within the next few years. That rise has sparked a [SELECT id=85] of research into how much of cancer is [SELECT id=86] our control and how much of it is simply a roll of the genetic dice.

​

Now, in an eye-opening study published in Science, researchers report that the majority of cancer types are the result of pure chance, the product of random genetic mutations that occur when stem cells make mistakes [SELECT id=87] the cell’s DNA.

​

Christian Tomasetti and Dr. Bert Vogelstein found that [SELECT id=88] stem cells there are in certain kind of tissues and [SELECT id=118] frequently they divide, [SELECT id=119] likely that that tissue is to develop cancer over a person’s lifetime. About 65% of cancers are the result of these DNA mistakes made by stem cells. Only a small proportion of a tissue’s cells are stem cells, which are essentially templates for making more tissue. The catch is that this kind of DNA copying is also the process behind cancer, which is triggered by cells that pick [SELECT id=89] mutations in the genes when they divide. The threat of cancer does not however mean you should stop [SELECT id=90] sunscreen or take up smoking. “The biggest fear is that people think they need do nothing. [SELECT id=91] is true,” says Tomasetti, who stresses that while we may not be able to prevent all tumors, we can focus on early detection and [SELECT id=92] lifesaving treatments. [CITE source=Time, 19 January 2015 (adapted)]

questions.main_text:
**European Maritime Safety Agency**

​

One of the main attributes of integrated maritime services is the ability to combine information from a range of different data sources, and as such greatly enrich the maritime domain awareness picture. These include data and operational functionalities directly contracted by EMSA: 

- Long range identification and tracking LRIT is a global ship identification and tracking system based on communications satellites. Under IMO regulations, cargo ships (300 tonnage and above), and mobile offshore drilling units on international voyages send mandatory position reports once every six hours. 

- SafeSeaNet system Member States also exchange a range of additional data through the SafeSeaNet system, including: hazardous material notifications and additional information sent in mandatory reporting areas.

- Synthetic aperture radar satellite images (SARDS) Satellite radar sensors measure the roughness of the sea surface independent of weather and sunlight conditions. On the satellite image, oil spills appear as dark areas, and vessels and platforms as bright spots. This is used in vessel detection systems (VDS) as well as pollution monitoring. [CITE source=http://emsa.europa.eu/ (8.5.2015, adapted)]

​

​

Under IMO regulations, cargo ships (300 tonnage and above), and mobile offshore drilling units on international voyages send mandatory position reports once every six hours. 

- SafeSeaNet system Member States also exchange a range of additional data through the SafeSeaNet system, including: hazardous material notifications and additional information sent in mandatory reporting areas.

- Synthetic aperture radar satellite images (SARDS) Satellite radar sensors measure the roughness of the sea surface independent of weather and sunlight conditions. On the satellite image, oil spills appear as dark areas, and vessels and platforms as bright spots. This is used in vessel detection systems (VDS) as well as pollution monitoring. questions.main_text:
**1.1a Meet a Success Coach**

​

As a Strayer University student, you’ll get a personal Success Coach to help with almost anything you need – in or out of the classroom. Your Coach is your main point of contact for educational, professional, and personal support. And all of our Success Coaches are perfect for the job. For example, Kathy Rollins is both a Success Coach and a Strayer student pursuing her MBA in Management. Kathy became a Success Coach to assist her fellow Strayer students on their journey as a major part of their support system. She’s a first-generation college graduate herself, so she knows firsthand how hard it can be to juggle both work and school full-time. Her objective is to ensure that her students never feel alone, are motivated to keep going no matter what, and always have the tools and resources needed to accomplish their goals. Kathy’s passion is her students’ success, and her students truly do their share as well. Open this flap to learn about one of Kathy’s favorite student success stories.

[CITE source=The Oprah Magazine, May 2015]

questions.main_text:
Although Oman shares many deeprooted cultural and religious ties with its neighbours on the Arabian Peninsula, it has its own unique history and geography that sets it firmly apart. To the trained eye, the turbans worn [SELECT id=93] many Omani men are an immediate clue to their nationality. The forts that overlook Muscat are permanent reminders of the country’s glory days when its trading empire stretched down the east coast of Africa [SELECT id=94] Zanzibar and eastward into modern-day Pakistan.

​

While pride in their history has helped give the Omani people a strong national identity, they are also ethnically diverse, a mixture of Arab clans cross-pollinated with the races they used to rule over in Pakistan, Africa and Iran. In recent years a new ingredient [SELECT id=95] into the melting pot and that is the dramatic shift in its demographic profile, which has seen the percentage of Omanis under the age of 25 increase to the [SELECT id=96] that they now represent over 50% of the population.

​

It is a demographic shift that [SELECT id=97] have a far-reaching implication for the country’s future and a challenge that the country [SELECT id=98] up to with both alacrity and enthusiasm. In the vanguard of this movement stands the Sultan Qaboos University. [SELECT id=99] in 1986, it has subsequently grown out of recognition in terms of students and volume and the variety of courses.

​

For [SELECT id=100] is not just its peoples’ ethnicities and the country’s overall history of empire that sets Oman apart from its northern neighbours on the peninsula. [SELECT id=101], the beauty and the diversity of terrain, climate, flora and fauna make Oman such a special country. [CITE source=National Geographic, November 2014 (adapted)]

​

questions.main_text:
**Churchhill**'**s reputation and India**

​

Winston Churchill opposed Gandhi’s peaceful disobedience revolt and the Indian Independence movement in the 1930s. Reports indicate that Churchill favoured [SELECT id=1] Gandhi die if he went on a hunger strike. During the first half of the 1930s, Churchill was outspoken in his opposition to granting Dominion status to India. He was a founder of the India Defence League, a group [SELECT id=78] to the preservation of British power in India. Churchill brooked no moderation. “[SELECT id=79]** **truth is,” he declared in 1930, “that Gandhi-ism and everything it stands [SELECT id=80]** **will have to be grappled with and crushed.” In speeches and press articles in this period, he forecast widespread unemployment in Britain and civil strife in India [SELECT id=81] be accorded. The Viceroy Lord Irwin, who had been appointed by the prior Conservative Government, engaged in the Round Table Conference in early 1931 and then announced the Government’s policy of granting India Dominion Status. At a meeting of the West Essex Conservative Association, specially [SELECT id=82] so that Churchill could explain his position, he said “It is alarming and also nauseating to see Mr Gandhi, a seditious Middle Temple lawyer, now posing as a fakir of a type well known in the East, striding half-naked up the steps of the Viceregal palace to negotiate [SELECT id=83]** **the representative of the King-Emperor.” [CITE source=www.chu.cam.ac.uk (10.3.2015, adapted)]

questions.main_text:
**Physical exercise at school **

​

Write your response to this discussion forum post in an online magazine Young Minds’ web site: 

​

_I love PE! It’s my favourite subject at school. I wish we had it every day. I’m really good at Finnish baseball. It’s so exciting and always a challenge. It also develops teamwork skills. Research shows that physical activity can boost self-esteem as well as reduce your risk of stress, depression, dementia and Alzheimer’s disease. If exercise were a pill, it would be one of the most cost-effective drugs ever invented. _– sporty Finn –

content.text:

​

​

questions.main_text:
**1.1d Rural Issues**

​

Llancarfan is just over ten miles from Cardiff’s multiplexes and arthouse cinemas, but that’s a long way when there’s limited public transport. For many village residents, the simple pleasure of watching a film with other people was an expensive treat until the Community Cinema started in 2012. What began as an idea brainstormed in the pub has evolved into a cherished amenity that unites generations and socio-economic groups in a way that no other type of attraction can. “The appeal of a film is universal,” says Jim Barratt, chair of the management committee. “In a village you often have a disparate range of people from different backgrounds who wouldn’t usually mix. The cinema provides an opportunity to go out and socialize.”

​

Monthly screenings are run by volunteers in the village hall, enhanced by interval drinks and themed food – Indian snacks during The Best Exotic Marigold Hotel, James Bond-inspired cocktails for Skyfall. What’s noteworthy is that a slide-show of residents’ photos and latest news always precedes each film, and special events have included a science-fiction weekend. “We work hard to match professional projection and sound standards so that people forget they’re in a village hall,” Jim says. “Our average audience size is 44. In a village of 200 residents, that’s not bad.” _(llancarfancinema.co.uk)_ [CITE source=Country Living, May 2015]

​

content.text:
**1.1d Rural Issues**

​

Llancarfan is just over ten miles from Cardiff’s multiplexes and arthouse cinemas, but that’s a long way when there’s limited public transport. For many village residents, the simple pleasure of watching a film with other people was an expensive treat until the Community Cinema started in 2012. What began as an idea brainstormed in the pub has evolved into a cherished amenity that unites generations and socio-economic groups in a way that no other type of attraction can. “The appeal of a film is universal,” says Jim Barratt, chair of the management committee. “In a village you often have a disparate range of people from different backgrounds who wouldn’t usually mix. The cinema provides an opportunity to go out and socialize.”

​

Monthly screenings are run by volunteers in the village hall, enhanced by interval drinks and themed food – Indian snacks during The Best Exotic Marigold Hotel, James Bond-inspired cocktails for Skyfall. What’s noteworthy is that a slide-show of residents’ photos and latest news always precedes each film, and special events have included a science-fiction weekend. “We work hard to match professional projection and sound standards so that people forget they’re in a village hall,” Jim says. “Our average audience size is 44. In a village of 200 residents, that’s not bad.” _(llancarfancinema.co.uk)_ [CITE source=Country Living, May 2015]

questions.main_text:
**1.1c The Rustout Syndrome**

​

“A subtle killer stalks America. This insidious force is more prevalent than heart disease, cancer, or alcoholism and yet little is done to prevent it or find a cure. We call it rustout. Rustout is the opposite of burnout. It is the slow death that follows when we stop making choices that keep life alive. It’s the feeling of numbness that comes from taking the safe way, never accepting new challenges, continually surrendering to the day-to-day routine. Rustout means we are no longer growing, but at best, are simply maintaining. It implies that we have traded the sensation of life for the security of a paycheck.” _Richard Leider and Steven Buchholz, “The Rustout Syndrome: A Personal and Organizational Perspective.”_ [CITE source=Harvard Business Review OnPoint, Spring 2015]

content.text:

​

“A subtle killer stalks America. This insidious force is more prevalent than heart disease, cancer, or alcoholism and yet little is done to prevent it or find a cure. We call it rustout. Rustout is the opposite of burnout. It is the slow death that follows when we stop making choices that keep life alive. It’s the feeling of numbness that comes from taking the safe way, never accepting new challenges, continually surrendering to the day-to-day routine. Rustout means we are no longer growing, but at best, are simply maintaining. It implies that we have traded the sensation of life for the security of a paycheck.” _Richard Leider and Steven Buchholz, “The Rustout Syndrome: A Personal and Organizational Perspective.”_ [CITE source=Harvard Business Review OnPoint, Spring 2015]
